formations_blocks: Czechia in a compact mid-low block
Korea building through possession from the back and midfield
Both teams initially resorting to direct long balls into the channels
adjustments: Korea briefly settled into keep-ball across the back five after a throw-in deep in Czechia's half
Korea shifted from direct channel attacks to combinations between the lines through Son, Lee Jae-sung and Lee Kang-in
key_matchups: Koreas long-ball attacks into the left channel against Czechia's back line
Korean playmakers Lee Kang-in, Son and Lee Jae-sung combining between the lines versus Czechia's central defenders
Son against the Czechia defensive line on runs into the left channel
phases.summary: The opening phase is defined by contrasting ideas: Czechia go direct immediately, while Korea begin in a compact mid-low block and look to combine through midfield when they get the ball. Much of the first 10 minutes is chaotic and aerial, with both sides using long balls into the channels, but Korea show more variety through Lee Kang-in and Son between the lines and on set-pieces, even if the delivery is inconsistent. Czechia’s best moment comes from their early physicality and directness, while Korea gradually settle into possession and create the first real save through cleaner midfield play.

formations_blocks: Korea in transition-focused shape, breaking quickly after defending set pieces
Czechia building attacks through left and right wide areas, with repeated corner pressure
adjustments: Short-corner routine from Korea to the penalty-spot area
Czechia varied corner delivery from right side, including switching to a left-footed inswinger after two outswingers
phases.summary: This phase opened up after an initially cautious feel, with Korea finding most of their joy in transition and Czechia relying more on sustained wide attacks and set pieces. Korea's short-corner routine created one dangerous moment, but Czechia soon imposed pressure through corners, particularly from the right side, where Soucek came closest to scoring.

adjustments: South Korea increasingly found joy in transition as the half wore on
South Korea worked the ball through Czechia’s midfield via Hwang In-beom and Paik Seung-ho to Son Heung-min
key_matchups: Hwang In-beom and Paik Seung-ho versus Czechia’s midfield
Son Heung-min receiving between the lines/at the feet against Czechia’s defence
South Korea’s resolute defence versus Czechia’s physical approach
phases.summary: South Korea were the better side at half-time, but the score remained 0-0 despite their growing control. Their main route forward became transition and progression through Czechia’s midfield via Hwang In-beom and Paik Seung-ho to Son Heung-min, while Czechia tried to assert themselves physically without creating much change against a disciplined Korean defence.

formations_blocks: South Korea working through midfield with sustained territorial control
adjustments: No changes from either side at half-time
Czechia dropped far too deep and stopped pressing the Korean midfield
Korea increased midfield circulation and used Son to turn and drive at goal
key_matchups: Hwang In-beom vs Czechia's deep defensive block
phases.summary: The second half began with no personnel or shape changes, but the game’s structure shifted quickly: Czechia sat increasingly deep and passive, while South Korea took control through tidy midfield circulation and direct running from Son. Korea created the clearer chances before and after the opener, yet Czechia’s late first goal and Korea’s equaliser in response underline how the match became a contest between Korean possession pressure and Czech resistance on the back foot.

formations_blocks: South Korea defending second balls after clearances
adjustments: South Korea made two substitutions at 82' (Park and Kim on)
South Korea shifted to calming the game down through midfield possession after taking the lead
key_matchups: Lee Han-beom vs Hlozek on the edge of the box
Kim Seung-gyu vs Hlozek at the far post
Korean defensive line vs Soucek's offside run
Hwang vs Czechia's left-sided defensive pressure in the build-up to Oh's goal
phases.summary: This phase was defined by set-piece threat versus Korean resilience, with Czechia repeatedly looking dangerous from long throws and direct deliveries while South Korea held a brave high line that narrowly caught Soucek offside. After Oh’s sliding finish put Korea 2-1 up, Czechia almost equalised immediately, but Kim Seung-gyu’s save and Korea’s ability to win second balls and clear pressure kept them in control. In the closing minutes South Korea used midfield possession to slow the game and pin Czechia back, leaving the visitors tired and unable to mount sustained attacks.
